map.subtitle: Unit 2: Securing Spaces   •   4 days   •   LO 2.3.A, 2.3.B
map.intro: Everything for this lesson lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Days 1–4 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, cold-call prompts, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with QOTD warm-ups, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Days 1–4 (student fill-in + answer KEY)

map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the daily instruction you project and teach from (Day 1 managerial controls, Day 2 the physical-control toolkit, Day 3 Scenario 2A application, Day 4 review and quiz).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.
Naming: the CED topic is 'Protecting Spaces'; the CED scenario is 'Scenario 2A: Securing Xtensr Labs'; the unit hub lists this lesson under 'Securing Spaces.' All refer to this lesson.

exercise1.title: Control Category Identifier
exercise1.intro: You are a physical security analyst reviewing measures proposed for a new building. For each measure you will name the control, decide whether it is a MANAGERIAL control (EK 2.3.A) or a PHYSICAL control (EK 2.3.B), cite the EK, and say what attack it addresses. Part A works the proposal table; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objectives 2.3.A and 2.3.B.
exercise1.scenarioTitle: Proposal Log: Five Proposed Security Measures
exercise1.narrative: A security technician submits five measures for the new lab building. Your job is to sort each one. Read the log row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: anchor your first instinct, decide the category (managerial = rules/training/policy per EK 2.3.A, or physical = hardware/barriers per EK 2.3.B), then match the control to the EK and the attack it stops.

rows: Require all employees to complete security awareness training
Teaches staff to detect phishing, refuse to badge others in, and prevent device theft
Every department, annually
A stranger was held the door into a restricted lab last month
Install bollards, fencing, and gates around the building
Deters and stops vehicles from reaching the structure
Building perimeter and lobby frontage
Cars can currently roll right up to the lobby glass wall
Add a privacy screen filter to each analyst workstation
Blocks others from viewing information on the screen
Open-plan desks near a busy walkway
Passersby have glanced at sensitive data on screens
Install card readers on restricted-area doors
Logs which badge opens which entry and denies unauthorized badges
All restricted-area entrances
There is no record of who enters restricted areas
Connect critical devices to a UPS and add a building generator
Supplies backup power to devices and a building section during outages
Server room and critical research systems
A power loss could drop critical research systems

exercise1.scenarioNote: Measures adapted from the Day 1 managerial-vs-physical enrichment and the Day 3 Scenario 2A worked table; control names and EKs follow the College Board AP Cybersecurity Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2026).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: which one measure is the trickiest to categorize, and what single detail decides whether it is managerial or physical?

partA.q: Measure 1 (security awareness training) is a managerial control. Name the EK, list the three behaviors the CED says this training targets, and explain why training — not a lock or fence — is the control that fits the door-holding concern in the log.
partA.answer: Measure 1 is security awareness training, a MANAGERIAL control under EK 2.3.A.1. The CED names exactly three behaviors it targets: detecting social engineering attempts like phishing; not badging other people into restricted areas (tailgating); and preventing device theft. It fits the door-holding concern because the stranger held into the restricted lab is a tailgating attempt — no lock or fence stops a tailgater the way a trained employee who politely refuses to hold the door does. Phishing and tailgating bypass technical controls by targeting the person, so the control must direct human behavior. Full credit requires naming the category (managerial), the EK, and all three behaviors.

partA.q: Measures 2 (bollards/fencing/gates) and 4 (card readers) are both PHYSICAL controls but serve different goals. Cite each EK, name the goal of each, and state whether each primarily prevents, detects, or corrects.
partA.answer: Both are physical controls under EK 2.3.B. Measure 2 — bollards, fencing, and gates (EK 2.3.B.2) — deters adversaries from physically reaching the building; its job is to PREVENT vehicle/perimeter access. Measure 4 — card readers (EK 2.3.B.4) — records which badge opens which entry at what time and denies unauthorized badges; it primarily DETECTS (it logs entries) and also prevents (it denies unauthorized badges). The contrast: B.2 keeps adversaries away from the perimeter, while B.4 creates an access record and gate at the door itself.

partA.q: Measure 3 (privacy screen filter) is hardware, yet the CED files it as part of a managerial control. Name the control and its EK, explain why a privacy screen counts as managerial here, and name the attack it defeats.
partA.answer: Measure 3 is a requirement of the workstation security policy, a MANAGERIAL control under EK 2.3.A.2. Even though a privacy screen filter is a physical object, the CED names it inside the workstation security POLICY, so in this context it is a requirement of a managerial control — the category is set by where the CED files it, not by whether the item is hardware. The privacy screen (or other physical barrier) prevents others from viewing information on the screen, which defeats shoulder-surfing by passersby near the busy walkway.

partA.q: Measure 5 (UPS + generator) addresses power loss. Cite its EK, state whether it prevents, detects, or corrects per the EK 2.3.B.1 reasoning, and explain why that justification — not just naming the control — is what a complete recommendation requires.
partA.answer: Measure 5 is a UPS for critical devices plus a building generator under EK 2.3.B.7. Per the EK 2.3.B.1 reasoning, it CORRECTS: it does not stop a power event, it keeps devices powered through an outage so research systems stay available and data is not lost. A complete recommendation must name the control AND say whether it prevents, detects, or corrects, because EK 2.3.B.1 requires reasoning about how the control answers the specific attack; a control named without a prevent/detect/correct justification is an incomplete answer.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: A hospital writes stricter desk rules for workstations handling patient records than for the lobby kiosk. Name the EK 2.3.A.2 feature this represents, define it, and give one other named requirement of a workstation security policy.
partB.answer: This is tiers of workstation security based on the type of data handled at a workstation (EK 2.3.A.2): a desk handling sensitive records follows stricter rules than a low-sensitivity kiosk. Another named requirement of a workstation security policy is locking devices before leaving them unattended (or clearing sensitive documents — the clean desk policy — or connecting devices to a surge protector/UPS). Any one of those named requirements earns credit.

partB.q: A team has more proposed controls than budget. According to EK 2.3.B.8, on what two factors should they base their prioritization, and why is a long unsorted list of every control an incomplete deliverable?
partB.answer: EK 2.3.B.8 says organizations prioritize risk mitigations based on the severity of the risks and the cost of the recommended mitigations. A long unsorted list is incomplete because the deliverable is a ranked plan: a severe, cheap fix (lock the server cabinet) should be funded before a low-severity, expensive one (a full perimeter generator). Recommending every control is fine; recommending them in a justified order by severity and cost is what the EK asks for.

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: Both halves of Lesson 2.3 are called 'physical security,' yet the CED splits the CONTROLS into two categories. Name the two categories, and explain the sorting rule that decides which a given measure belongs to.
partB.answer: The two categories are managerial controls (EK 2.3.A — rules, policy, and training that direct people, like security awareness training and the workstation security policy) and physical controls (EK 2.3.B — hardware and barriers like fences, locks, and card readers). The sorting rule: ask whether the control is a rule/policy/training that a person carries out, or a standalone physical object doing its job whether or not anyone is trained — and note where the CED files it (a privacy screen inside the workstation policy is managerial in that context). A strong answer names the category first, then the specific control.

exercise2.title: Xtensr Labs Security Advisor
exercise2.subtitle: 3 gaps - Scenario 2A
exercise2.intro: You are on the physical security team at Xtensr Research Labs. Three gaps at the newly acquired lab need a recommendation. For each, identify the adversary move, recommend the CB control BY NAME AND EK, and state whether it prevents, detects, or corrects. Advanced students also name the second control they would layer on the same gap. Vague 'add more security' advice scores zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): adversary move + named EK 2.3.B control + prevent/detect/correct, for all 3 gaps. Enrichment (optional): also name a second control to layer (managerial or physical) for more complete mitigation.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every gap
method: Anchor your gut read of the gap before analyzing. Writing your own answer first keeps a convincing wrong option from swaying you.

State how an adversary would exploit the vulnerability, then ask whether a control would prevent, detect, or correct that attack. Match the control to the mechanism — a bollard does nothing for shoulder-surfing.
Recommend the specific CB control (EK 2.3.B.2–B.7) and cite its EK. A control named without a prevent/detect/correct justification is an incomplete answer.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (layering and prioritization): Strong Scenario 2A answers combine controls on one gap — a vestibule AND training for a propped door (EK 2.3.B.5 + 2.3.A.1); a cabinet lock AND a card-reader log for the server room (EK 2.3.B.3 + 2.3.B.4). When the budget is finite, rank mitigations by severity and cost (EK 2.3.B.8): a severe, cheap fix comes before a low-severity, expensive one.
fields: Adversary move (how the gap is exploited):

scenarios.text: Scenario 2A — Xtensr's new lab has a side fire door that staff prop open with a chair, so anyone can walk into the restricted interior unchallenged. The physical security team must recommend a control to mitigate this gap.

key.signs: Adversary move: tailgate in behind staff, or simply walk through the propped door unchallenged into the restricted area — no badge or verification is enforced at this entry.
key.mechanism: Recommend an access control vestibule or turnstile at that entry (EK 2.3.B.5). It PREVENTS an authorized person from intentionally or accidentally admitting an unauthorized person into the restricted area, which is exactly the propped-door problem.
key.subtype: Layer security awareness training (EK 2.3.A.1) so staff stop propping the door in the first place — a managerial + physical pair that mitigates the gap more completely than the vestibule alone.

scenarios.text: Xtensr's server room rack cabinet has no lock, and there is no record of who enters the room. An intruder reaching the rack could pull or tamper with a drive, and the team would have no log of who was inside.

key.signs: Adversary move: reach the unlocked rack and remove or tamper with a drive or device; because nothing logs entry, the team cannot tell who accessed the room or when.
key.mechanism: Recommend locks on the server cabinet (and the room door and computers) under EK 2.3.B.3 — this PREVENTS the devices from being accessed or stolen. The lock directly closes the unlocked-cabinet gap.
key.subtype: Layer card readers on the room door (EK 2.3.B.4) to DETECT access by logging which badge entered at what time and denying unauthorized badges — lock to prevent, card reader to detect and record.

scenarios.text: On Xtensr's open-plan floor, USB drives are freely used on shared computers, and the lab also worries a power loss could drop critical research systems mid-experiment. The team can only justify the controls that match each specific risk.

key.signs: Adversary move (USB): an insider or visitor plugs an external drive into a shared computer and loads malware. Separate risk (power): an outage drops critical systems, causing downtime and possible data loss — not an adversary action, but a real availability threat.
key.mechanism: For the USB risk, disable the USB ports on those computers (EK 2.3.B.6) — this PREVENTS external drives from loading malware. For the power risk, connect critical devices to a UPS and add a generator (EK 2.3.B.7) — this CORRECTS by keeping devices powered through an outage. Each control matches its specific risk; a UPS does nothing about USB malware and vice versa.
key.subtype: Prioritize by severity and cost (EK 2.3.B.8): disabling USB ports is cheap and addresses a malware-introduction risk, so fund it early; a full building generator is costly and may rank lower if the outage risk is low-likelihood. Trap: recommending every control without ordering them by severity and cost is an incomplete deliverable.

discussion.title: Protecting the Space
discussion.minutes: ~25 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 2) + Closing (Day 3)
discussion.overview: A building can have the best card readers money can buy, and one untrained employee holding a door defeats them instantly — while a clean desk policy and a locked screen stop an intruder already inside that no fence can touch. Across three short windows in this lesson, your group will connect the CB managerial and physical controls to real life, debate which control is hardest for a real organization to actually sustain, and decide how a defender should reason from the adversary's side and prioritize a finite budget on Scenario 2A.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. The phrase 'physical security' makes people assume the controls must be physical objects — locks, fences, cameras. Why do training and written policy feel like 'soft' measures, and what would it actually take to get people to treat refusing to hold a door as a real security control (not just 'be polite about it')?

Question 2. Whose job is protecting a workplace — the employees who follow (or ignore) the rules, or the organization that installs the hardware? Defend your split with one concrete example, such as a tailgater at a badge reader or a client list left on an unlocked desk.
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, right after the physical-control toolkit debrief
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - Adversary-first reasoning  [EK 2.3.B.1]
items.text: EK 2.3.B.1 says a defender first asks how an adversary would exploit a vulnerability, then whether a control would prevent, detect, or correct it. In one sentence your whole group agrees on, explain why reasoning from the attacker's side picks better controls than starting from a catalog of hardware.

items.label: Task B - Which control is hardest to live with?  [EK 2.3.A]
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: of the managerial controls — security awareness training that stops tailgating (EK 2.3.A.1) and a workstation security policy with a clean desk and locked screens (EK 2.3.A.2) — which is hardest for a normal employee to actually do every single day, and what is the realistic cost of skipping it? Record your group's ranking and one reason.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 3, ~9 min, after the Scenario 2A worked table
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The 'fund everything' case
items.text: Xtensr Labs has gaps at every turn — vehicles reaching the lobby glass, a propped fire door, an unlocked server cabinet, free USB use, and a power-loss risk — but a finite budget (Day 3 Scenario 2A). Design the plan: which single gap would you fund FIRST and why, how does EK 2.3.B.8 (severity and cost) justify your order, and where would you LAYER a managerial control on a physical one for more complete mitigation?
Our group's verdict (first-funded control + one-sentence justification by severity and cost):
